card.title: Can a treasure hunt fix a broken loyalty loop?
card.subtitle: A B2B2C gaming platform that turns Taco Bell's loyalty program into a real-world treasure hunt.

sections.title: Can a treasure hunt fix a broken loyalty loop?
sections.subtitle: Taco Bell's Rewards program had 8M active users and a retention problem. I designed a B2B2C gaming platform to give users a reason to come back.

sections.label: THE PROBLEM
sections.title: 29M users downloaded Taco Bell's app, but only 16% came back for more.
sections.body: The app was supposed to drive store visits but the users left after their first free taco. Treasure Game$, a phygital gaming company, saw an opportunity: what if the reward was a real prize worth chasing with friends, one that sent you into the world to find it?

cards.label: of subscribers churn after their first free taco.
on Google Play (one of the lowest scores in the QSR category.)

sections.label: BENCHMARK
sections.title: Every QSR that gamified their app pulled millions in weeks.
sections.body: McDonald's Monopoly drove 77M digital interactions, a 25% spike in app downloads, and a +15% sales lift in six weeks. Chipotle's Burrito Vault pulled 3.5M plays in 2025. Burger King's Whopper Detour drove 1.5M app downloads in three days.

That's how the Golden Taco Treasure Hunt came to life: 10 Golden Tacos worth $10K each, hidden across the country. Players move through worlds, levels, and clues, solving some at home and others inside Taco Bell stores, turning every location into part of the game.

sections.label: USER INTERVIEWS
sections.title: What Wish Lamp$ players taught us.
sections.body: To validate our assumptions, I interviewed 9 first-time players of Wish Lamp$: Treasure Games's flagship treasure hunt and the closest proxy to our future Taco Bell audience.

sections.title: Onboarding one player means recruiting a whole team.
sections.body: Players tried the game solo first and only invited others once convinced of the value. A confusing onboarding didn't just lose one player — it prevented an entire team from forming.

sections.title: Teams felt like a feature. Players told us they were the whole point.
sections.body: Bonding across states, generations, and busy schedules was the core reason people played at all. This pushed us to ship deeper team features from day one.

sections.title: The competition wasn't other games. It was the couch.
sections.body: Players mostly swapped passive screen time for Wish Lamp$. What made the switch stick was the social and physical twist: something familiar, made worth sharing.
